title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 10 (Fragment 3)
text:
Maybe he was in earnest. It would suit Rinaldo to work in secret, with lots of willing helpers. But Paolo was sure that this plan was a step in Rinaldo’s plans to be head of the Casa. Paolo sidled away along the rail.

“Are you game?” Rinaldo whispered, laughing a little.

Paolo sidled beyond grabbing-distance. “Tell you later.” He turned and scudded away. Rinaldo laughed and did not try to catch him. He thought Paolo was scared.

Paolo went down into the yard, feeling more lonely than he had felt in his life. Tonino was not there. Tonino was not vain, or selfish, or cowardly. And nobody would help him find Tonino. Paolo had not noticed until now how much he depended on Tonino. They did everything important together. Even if Paolo was busy on his own, he knew Tonino was there somewhere, sitting reading, ready to put his book down if Paolo needed him. Now there seemed to be nothing for Paolo to do. And the whole Casa reeked of worry.

He went to the kitchen, where there seemed, at last, to be something happening. All his small cousins were there. Rosa and Marco were trying to make soup for them.

“Come in and help, Paolo,” Rosa said. “We’re going to put them to bed after soup, but we’re having a bit of trouble.”

Both she and Marco were looking tired and flustered. Most of the little ones were grizzling, including the baby. The trouble was Lucia’s spell. Paolo understood this because Marco dumped the baby in his arms. Its wrapper was covered with orange grease. “Yuk!” said Paolo.

“I know,” said Rosa. “Well, Marco, better try again. Clean saucepan. Clean water. The very last packet of soup powder—don’t make that face, Paolo. We’ve got through all the vegetables. They just sail away to the waste-bins, and they’re moldy before they get there.”

Paolo looked nervously at the door, wondering if the enemy enchanter was powerful enough to overhear him. “Try a cancel-spell,” he whispered.

“Aunt Gina went through them all this afternoon,” said Rosa. “No good. Little Lucia used the Angel of Caprona , you see. We’re trying Marco’s way now. Ready, Marco?”

Rosa opened the packet of soup and held it over the saucepan. As the dry pink powder poured into the water, Marco leaned over the saucepan and sang furiously. Paolo watched them nervously. This was just what the message told them not to do, he was sure. When all the powder was in the water, Rosa and Marco peered anxiously into the saucepan. “Have we done it?” asked Marco.

“I think—” Rosa began, and ended in a yell of exasperation. “Oh no!” The little pasta shells in the powder had turned into real sea-shells, little gray ones. “With creatures in!” Rosa said despairingly, dipping a spoonful out. “Where is Lucia?” she said. “Bring her here. Tell her I—No, don’t. Just fetch her, Paolo.”

“She’s gone to the chemist,” said Paolo.

There was shouting in the yard. Paolo passed the greasy baby to the nearest cousin and shot outside, dreading another sick yellow message about Tonino. Or there was just a chance the noise was Lucia.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell used to negate or nullify another spell.
vocabulary.example: Try a cancel-spell.

vocabulary.definition: A person or shop selling medicinal drugs.
vocabulary.example: She’s gone to the chemist.

vocabulary.definition: Showing a lack of courage; excessively afraid of danger or pain.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was not vain, or selfish, or cowardly.

vocabulary.definition: To be influenced or determined by something else; to rely on.
vocabulary.example: Paolo had not noticed until now how much he depended on Tonino.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing hopelessness or gloom.
vocabulary.example: Rosa said despairingly, dipping a spoonful out.

vocabulary.definition: To anticipate with great fear or apprehension.
vocabulary.example: He shot outside, dreading another sick yellow message about Tonino.

vocabulary.definition: Serious and sincere in intention or effort.
vocabulary.example: Maybe he was in earnest.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or enchanting spells.
vocabulary.example: Wondering if the enemy enchanter was powerful enough to overhear him.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of intense irritation or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: Rosa began, and ended in a yell of exasperation.

vocabulary.definition: To go for and then bring back something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Just fetch her, Paolo.

vocabulary.definition: Nervous and confused, agitated as a result of pressure or stress.
vocabulary.example: Both she and Marco were looking tired and flustered.

vocabulary.definition: Within reach; close enough to be grabbed or taken.
vocabulary.example: Paolo sidled beyond grabbing-distance.

vocabulary.definition: A thick oily substance, especially as used for lubrication or cooking.
vocabulary.example: Its wrapper was covered with orange grease.

vocabulary.definition: To complain or whine persistently.
vocabulary.example: Most of the little ones were grizzling, including the baby.

vocabulary.definition: Covered with or smelling of mold.
vocabulary.example: They just sail away to the waste-bins, and they’re moldy before they get there.

vocabulary.definition: To hear something without the speaker's intention or knowledge.
vocabulary.example: Wondering if the enemy enchanter was powerful enough to overhear him.

vocabulary.definition: A spice used for seasoning food (contextual, not in text but common word).
To have a strong, unpleasant smell.
vocabulary.example: And the whole Casa reeked of worry.

vocabulary.definition: (Of food) to slide smoothly out of the mouth or onto the waste, especially unnoticed or wasted.
vocabulary.example: They just sail away to the waste-bins.

vocabulary.definition: A deep cooking pan with a handle used for boiling or simmering food.
vocabulary.example: Clean saucepan. Clean water.

vocabulary.definition: To move fast in a straight line because of the wind or haste.
vocabulary.example: He turned and scudded away.

vocabulary.definition: Lacking consideration for others; concerned chiefly with one's own personal profit or pleasure.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was not vain, or selfish, or cowardly.

vocabulary.definition: To walk in a furtive, unobtrusive, or timid manner, especially sideways or obliquely.
vocabulary.example: Paolo sidled away along the rail.

vocabulary.definition: Having an acid taste like lemon or vinegar (contextual, not in text but relevant to taste-related words).
Having or showing an excessively high opinion of one's appearance, abilities, or worth.
vocabulary.example: Tonino was not vain, or selfish, or cowardly.

vocabulary.definition: To speak very softly using one's breath without vocal cords.
vocabulary.example: Rinaldo whispered, laughing a little.

vocabulary.definition: A piece of material or paper that covers and protects something.
vocabulary.example: Its wrapper was covered with orange grease.
